topics.title: ST2134 — Minimal Sufficiency: Lehmann–Scheffé Worked Examples

sections.0:
A **sufficient statistic** keeps everything in the data that is informative about θ. But there can be many sufficient statistics — the entire sample is trivially sufficient, and so is any one-to-one transformation of any sufficient statistic. The question becomes: what is the *most compressed* sufficient statistic? That is the **minimal sufficient statistic** — a sufficient statistic that is a function of every other sufficient statistic.

To check whether a candidate T is minimal sufficient, you can use a clean criterion due to Lehmann and Scheffé. Look at the **likelihood ratio** L(θ; x) / L(θ; y) for two sample points x and y. If this ratio is **independent of θ exactly when T(x) = T(y)**, then T is minimal sufficient. Intuitively: two samples that produce the same value of T are "indistinguishable" from the parameter's point of view, and samples that produce different values of T are distinguishable. T captures every distinction the parameter can detect, and no more.

The most common pattern in exam questions is the **exponential family**. Distributions like Bernoulli, Poisson, Normal, Gamma, and Beta have densities that can be written

f(x; θ) = h(x) · c(θ) · exp(η(θ)' T(x)).

In an exponential family, T(X) = (Σᵢ T₁(Xᵢ), ..., Σᵢ Tₖ(Xᵢ)) is **always** minimal sufficient for the natural parameter — the Lehmann–Scheffé criterion is automatically satisfied.

The trickier cases are non-exponential-family distributions, especially when the **support depends on the parameter** (e.g. U(0, θ)). For those you have to compute the likelihood ratio directly, often involving indicator functions, and read off T from the condition that the ratio is parameter-free.
sections.1:
### Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Sufficient statistic** | T(X) is sufficient for θ if the conditional distribution of X given T(X) does not depend on θ. |
| **Minimal sufficient** | T is minimal sufficient if T is sufficient and is a function of every other sufficient statistic S (i.e. T = ψ(S) for some ψ). |
| **Complete** | T is complete if E_θ[g(T)] = 0 for all θ implies g(T) = 0 a.s. |
| **Lehmann–Scheffé characterisation** | T is minimal sufficient ⇔ for any two sample points x, y, L(θ; x)/L(θ; y) is free of θ ⇔ T(x) = T(y). |

### Why we want minimal sufficiency

- Minimum-data summary that loses nothing about θ.
- Required ingredient (with completeness) in **Lehmann–Scheffé theorem**: any unbiased estimator that is a function of a complete sufficient statistic is the **UMVUE**.
- Rao–Blackwell + completeness + minimality → uniqueness of the best unbiased estimator.

### Lehmann–Scheffé criterion (likelihood ratio version)

**Theorem.** Suppose for any two samples x and y the ratio

L(θ; x) / L(θ; y)

is constant in θ if and only if T(x) = T(y). Then T is minimal sufficient for θ.

**Sketch of proof.** Sufficiency follows because the ratio's θ-independence implies the conditional distribution of X given T does not depend on θ (this is the factorisation theorem viewed sideways). Minimality follows because any other sufficient statistic S induces the same equivalence classes on the sample space — so T must be a function of S.

### Exponential family auto-result

If f(x; θ) = h(x) c(θ) exp(η(θ)' T₀(x)) and η(θ) varies in a non-degenerate way (the exponential family is "regular" / "full rank"), then T(X) = Σᵢ T₀(Xᵢ) is a minimal sufficient statistic. In that case it is also **complete**, so Lehmann–Scheffé applies and any unbiased function of T is UMVUE.

| Distribution | Natural sufficient T(X) | Complete? |
|---|---|---|
| Bernoulli(π) | Σ Xᵢ | Yes |
| Poisson(λ) | Σ Xᵢ | Yes |
| N(μ, σ²) (μ unknown, σ² known) | Σ Xᵢ | Yes |
| N(μ, σ²) (both unknown) | (Σ Xᵢ, Σ Xᵢ²) | Yes |
| Exp(λ) | Σ Xᵢ | Yes |
| Gamma(α, β) | (Σ Xᵢ, Σ log Xᵢ) | Yes |
| Beta(α, β) | (Σ log Xᵢ, Σ log(1−Xᵢ)) | Yes |

### Non-exponential / support-dependent distributions

When the support depends on θ, the distribution is **not** in the exponential family and minimality must be checked directly.

| Distribution | Minimal sufficient T(X) |
|---|---|
| Uniform[0, θ] | X_{(n)} |
| Uniform[θ, θ+1] | (X_{(1)}, X_{(n)}) — bivariate |
| Shifted Exp(λ, θ): λ e^{−λ(x−θ)}, x ≥ θ | (X_{(1)}, Σ Xᵢ) |
| Cauchy(θ, 1) | The full order statistics (X_{(1)}, ..., X_{(n)}) — no further reduction! |

**Cauchy lesson.** Some distributions admit no useful reduction beyond the order statistics; the entire sorted sample is minimal sufficient. This is a counter-example to the comforting intuition that minimal sufficient statistics are always low-dimensional.

### Relationship to other concepts

- **Sufficient ⇒ minimal sufficient ⇒ complete ⇒ UMVUE.** Each step requires more.
- The MLE (when unique) is always a function of any sufficient statistic, in particular the minimal sufficient one.
- Ancillary statistics (independent of θ) are uninformative and never feature in a minimal sufficient T.
sections.2:
| concept | formula |
|---|---|
| Likelihood | L(θ; x) = ∏ᵢ f(xᵢ; θ) |
| Factorisation theorem | T sufficient ⇔ f(x;θ) = g(T(x), θ) h(x) |
| Lehmann–Scheffé characterisation | T minimal sufficient ⇔ [L(θ;x)/L(θ;y) free of θ ⇔ T(x) = T(y)] |
| Exponential family form | f(x;θ) = h(x) c(θ) exp(η(θ)' T(x)) |
| Exp. family natural sufficient stat | T(X) = (Σᵢ T₁(Xᵢ), ..., Σᵢ Tₖ(Xᵢ)) |
| Completeness | E_θ[g(T)] = 0 ∀θ ⇒ g(T) = 0 a.s. |
| Lehmann–Scheffé UMVUE | T complete sufficient + h(T) unbiased ⇒ h(T) is UMVUE |
| Rao–Blackwell | If τ̃ unbiased and T sufficient, then E[τ̃ | T] is unbiased with smaller variance |

### Lehmann–Scheffé recipe

```
GIVEN: candidate T(X) and a parametric family f(x; θ).

STEP 1. Write the likelihood L(θ; x) (with all indicator/support terms).
STEP 2. Form the ratio L(θ; x) / L(θ; y).
STEP 3. Identify the function of x and y under which the ratio is constant in θ.
STEP 4. Check that "ratio free of θ" ⇔ "T(x) = T(y)".
        - "⇐": if T(x) = T(y), substitute and show ratio cancels.
        - "⇒": assume ratio free of θ; show this forces T(x) = T(y).
STEP 5. Conclude that T is minimal sufficient by the Lehmann–Scheffé criterion.
```
sections.3:
### Example A — Normal(μ, σ²), both parameters unknown

**Setup.** X₁, ..., Xₙ ~iid N(μ, σ²), parameters θ = (μ, σ²) ∈ R × (0, ∞).

**Likelihood.**

L(θ; x) = (2πσ²)^{−n/2} exp[−(1/(2σ²)) Σ(xᵢ − μ)²]
       = (2πσ²)^{−n/2} exp[−(1/(2σ²)) (Σ xᵢ² − 2μ Σ xᵢ + nμ²)].

**Likelihood ratio.**

L(θ; x) / L(θ; y) = exp[(1/(2σ²)) {(Σ yᵢ² − Σ xᵢ²) + 2μ(Σ xᵢ − Σ yᵢ)}].

**Step 4 (the key step).** This ratio is free of σ² ⇔ Σ xᵢ² = Σ yᵢ² **and** the coefficient of μ vanishes ⇔ Σ xᵢ = Σ yᵢ.

(In more detail: for the ratio to be independent of σ², the entire exponent must be zero, since σ² ranges over (0, ∞). For the ratio also to be free of μ, the coefficient of μ must vanish, i.e. Σ xᵢ = Σ yᵢ. Given that, the σ²-coefficient becomes Σ yᵢ² − Σ xᵢ², which must also vanish.)

**Conclusion.** L(θ; x)/L(θ; y) is free of θ ⇔ Σ xᵢ = Σ yᵢ **and** Σ xᵢ² = Σ yᵢ². Hence

T(X) = (Σ Xᵢ, Σ Xᵢ²)

is minimal sufficient for (μ, σ²). Equivalently, (X̄, S²) is minimal sufficient (one-to-one with T).

**Connection.** Normal(μ, σ²) is an exponential family with natural parameters (μ/σ², −1/(2σ²)) and natural sufficient statistic (Σ Xᵢ, Σ Xᵢ²). The Lehmann–Scheffé argument here just confirms the exponential-family auto-result.

### Example B — Exponential family (one-parameter)

**Setup.** X₁, ..., Xₙ ~iid f(x; θ) = h(x) c(θ) exp(η(θ) T₀(x)) where η : Θ → R is one-to-one (i.e. the family is identifiable in η).

**Likelihood.**

L(θ; x) = (∏ h(xᵢ)) c(θ)ⁿ exp(η(θ) Σ T₀(xᵢ)).

**Likelihood ratio.**

L(θ; x) / L(θ; y) = (∏ h(xᵢ) / ∏ h(yᵢ)) exp(η(θ) [Σ T₀(xᵢ) − Σ T₀(yᵢ)]).

**Step 4.** The first factor is free of θ. The exponential factor is free of θ ⇔ η(θ) [Σ T₀(xᵢ) − Σ T₀(yᵢ)] does not depend on θ. Since η(θ) varies, this requires Σ T₀(xᵢ) = Σ T₀(yᵢ).

**Conclusion.** T(X) = Σᵢ T₀(Xᵢ) is minimal sufficient for θ. This is the general fact: in any **regular full-rank exponential family**, the natural statistic is minimal sufficient.

**Specialisation — Bernoulli(π).** f(x; π) = π^x (1 − π)^{1−x} = (1 − π) exp(log(π/(1−π)) · x). Here T₀(x) = x and η(π) = log(π/(1−π)). Hence **Σ Xᵢ is minimal sufficient for π**, recovering the familiar coin-flip example.

### Example C — Uniform U(0, θ) (support depends on θ)

**Setup.** X₁, ..., Xₙ ~iid Uniform(0, θ), θ > 0.

**Likelihood.**

L(θ; x) = θ^{−n} · ∏ᵢ I(0 ≤ xᵢ ≤ θ) = θ^{−n} · I(x_{(1)} ≥ 0) · I(x_{(n)} ≤ θ).

(For x in the support 0 ≤ all xᵢ, the indicator I(x_{(1)} ≥ 0) = 1.)

**Likelihood ratio.**

L(θ; x) / L(θ; y) = I(x_{(n)} ≤ θ) / I(y_{(n)} ≤ θ),  for x, y in the positive orthant.

This ratio is 0/0 or 0/1 or 1/0 or 1/1 depending on θ. The ratio is **well-defined and free of θ** for all θ ≥ max(x_{(n)}, y_{(n)}), where it equals 1, **and the ratio of indicator functions across all θ** is identically 1 only if x_{(n)} = y_{(n)} (so the cut-offs coincide).

**Step 4.** Treating the ratio as a function of θ on (0, ∞):

- If x_{(n)} = y_{(n)} = a, both numerator and denominator switch from 0 to 1 at θ = a. So the ratio is 0/0 for θ < a (undefined) and 1 for θ ≥ a — free of θ on its domain of definition.
- If x_{(n)} ≠ y_{(n)}, say x_{(n)} < y_{(n)}, then for θ in (x_{(n)}, y_{(n)}) the ratio equals 1/0 = ∞, while for θ ≥ y_{(n)} it equals 1. The ratio depends on θ.

**Conclusion.** Free of θ ⇔ x_{(n)} = y_{(n)}. Therefore **T(X) = X_{(n)}** is minimal sufficient for θ. (In fact it is also complete, so it is the UMVUE-generating statistic.)

**Bias.** E[X_{(n)}] = nθ/(n+1), so the unbiased corrected estimator (n+1)X_{(n)}/n is the UMVUE for θ by Lehmann–Scheffé.

### Example D — Uniform U(θ, θ + 1) (location uniform)

**Setup.** X₁, ..., Xₙ ~iid Uniform(θ, θ + 1), θ ∈ R.

**Likelihood.**

L(θ; x) = ∏ I(θ ≤ xᵢ ≤ θ + 1) = I(θ ≤ x_{(1)} ≤ x_{(n)} ≤ θ + 1) = I(x_{(n)} − 1 ≤ θ ≤ x_{(1)}).

**Likelihood ratio.**

L(θ; x) / L(θ; y) = I(x_{(n)} − 1 ≤ θ ≤ x_{(1)}) / I(y_{(n)} − 1 ≤ θ ≤ y_{(1)}).

This is constant in θ on the overlap of the two intervals (both equal to 1) and equal to 0 or ∞ elsewhere. The ratio is free of θ for all θ ⇔ both intervals coincide, i.e. **x_{(1)} = y_{(1)} and x_{(n)} = y_{(n)}**.

**Conclusion.** T(X) = (X_{(1)}, X_{(n)}) is minimal sufficient for θ. The minimal sufficient statistic is **two-dimensional even though θ is one-dimensional** — a famous illustration that minimal sufficiency need not match parameter dimension. (T is **not** complete.)
sections.4:
1. **(8 min)** **Task 1.** State the definition of a minimal sufficient statistic. State the Lehmann–Scheffé likelihood-ratio characterisation. Explain in one sentence why the criterion proves both sufficiency and minimality.

2. **(10 min)** **Task 2.** Show that for a regular exponential family f(x; θ) = h(x)c(θ) exp(η(θ) T₀(x)) the statistic T(X) = Σ T₀(Xᵢ) is minimal sufficient for θ.

3. **(12 min)** **Task 3.** Let X₁, ..., Xₙ ~iid N(μ, σ²), both parameters unknown. Use the Lehmann–Scheffé criterion to show (Σ Xᵢ, Σ Xᵢ²) is minimal sufficient for (μ, σ²).

4. **(12 min)** **Task 4.** Let X₁, ..., Xₙ ~iid Uniform(0, θ). Use the Lehmann–Scheffé criterion to show X_{(n)} is minimal sufficient. Be explicit about the indicator functions and how the ratio is interpreted.

5. **(8 min)** **Task 5.** Let X₁, ..., Xₙ ~iid Uniform(θ, θ + 1). Show that the minimal sufficient statistic is two-dimensional, namely (X_{(1)}, X_{(n)}), even though θ is one-dimensional. Comment on what this implies about the relationship between minimal sufficient statistic dimension and parameter dimension.
sections.5:
### For "show T is minimal sufficient" questions

```
1. Write the likelihood L(θ; x) including indicator/support terms.
2. Form the ratio L(θ; x) / L(θ; y).
3. Simplify; identify which functions of (x, y) appear.
4. Argue both directions:
   (i) T(x) = T(y) ⇒ ratio is free of θ;
   (ii) ratio free of θ ⇒ T(x) = T(y).
   - For exponential families (i) is by substitution
     and (ii) uses η(θ) varying.
   - For support-dependent families (i) and (ii) come from
     comparing indicator functions.
5. Conclude: by the Lehmann–Scheffé criterion, T(X) is minimal sufficient.
6. (If asked) note completeness and the resulting UMVUE.
```

### For exponential-family auto-result

```
1. Identify the family form: f(x;θ) = h(x)c(θ)exp(η(θ)T₀(x)).
2. State that η is one-to-one / parameter not lying in a lower-dim subspace.
3. Quote: "By Lehmann–Scheffé, T(X) = Σ T₀(Xᵢ) is minimal sufficient."
4. Add: "And complete, so any unbiased function of T is UMVUE."
```
sections.6:
1. **Confusing minimal sufficient with complete.** Minimal ≠ complete. (X_{(1)}, X_{(n)}) for Uniform(θ, θ+1) is minimal sufficient but not complete — examples like X_{(n)} − X_{(1)} − (n−1)/(n+1) are unbiased estimators of 0. State the distinction explicitly.

2. **Forgetting the ⇐ direction.** Showing the ratio is free of θ when T(x) = T(y) is the easy half. The hard direction is the converse: if the ratio is free of θ, then T(x) = T(y). Examiners look for both directions.

3. **Skipping indicator functions for support-dependent distributions.** Without I(x_{(n)} ≤ θ) you cannot tell where the support cut-off lies, and the ratio argument breaks. This is the same trap that bites in factorisation-theorem questions.

4. **Treating the LR ratio "for one specific θ".** The criterion requires the ratio to be free of **all** θ in the parameter space simultaneously. A ratio that happens to equal 1 for one θ but varies elsewhere is not free of θ.

5. **Confusing minimal sufficiency dimension with parameter dimension.** Minimal sufficient T can have higher (or lower) dimension than θ. Uniform(θ, θ+1) has θ ∈ R (1-d) but minimal sufficient (X_{(1)}, X_{(n)}) (2-d). Cauchy has 1-d θ but the entire order statistics as minimal sufficient.

6. **Using "minimal" when you mean "complete sufficient."** The Lehmann–Scheffé theorem about UMVUEs requires **complete** sufficient. Don't quote "minimal sufficient" alone and conclude UMVUE — you need completeness too.

7. **Failing to invoke the natural-parameter property in exponential families.** The auto-result requires that η(θ) varies (the family is full-rank). For curved exponential families (η constrained to a sub-manifold), the dimension can collapse and the natural T need not be minimal.

8. **Using the unbiased estimator as the minimal sufficient statistic.** The MLE or UMVUE need not be the *statistic* T; they are *functions* of T. State T (e.g. (Σ Xᵢ, Σ Xᵢ²)) as the minimal sufficient and identify estimators as functions of it.

9. **Saying "T is minimal sufficient because it is the lowest-dimensional sufficient statistic."** Dimension alone does not certify minimality. Use the Lehmann–Scheffé criterion or quote the exponential-family auto-result.

10. **Over-answering "state the criterion" questions.** If asked only to state Lehmann–Scheffé, write the criterion cleanly; do not launch into an example unless prompted. Marks are awarded for precision, not length.

formulas.formula: T sufficient ⇔ f(x;θ) = g(T(x), θ) h(x)
T minimal sufficient ⇔ [L(θ;x)/L(θ;y) free of θ ⇔ T(x) = T(y)]
T complete sufficient + h(T) unbiased ⇒ h(T) is UMVUE
If τ̃ unbiased and T sufficient, then E[τ̃ | T] is unbiased with smaller variance

questions.text: **Task 1.** State the definition of a minimal sufficient statistic. State the Lehmann–Scheffé likelihood-ratio characterisation. Explain in one sentence why the criterion proves both sufficiency and minimality.
**Task 2.** Show that for a regular exponential family f(x; θ) = h(x)c(θ) exp(η(θ) T₀(x)) the statistic T(X) = Σ T₀(Xᵢ) is minimal sufficient for θ.
**Task 3.** Let X₁, ..., Xₙ ~iid N(μ, σ²), both parameters unknown. Use the Lehmann–Scheffé criterion to show (Σ Xᵢ, Σ Xᵢ²) is minimal sufficient for (μ, σ²).
**Task 4.** Let X₁, ..., Xₙ ~iid Uniform(0, θ). Use the Lehmann–Scheffé criterion to show X_{(n)} is minimal sufficient. Be explicit about the indicator functions and how the ratio is interpreted.

**Task 5.** Let X₁, ..., Xₙ ~iid Uniform(θ, θ + 1). Show that the minimal sufficient statistic is two-dimensional, namely (X_{(1)}, X_{(n)}), even though θ is one-dimensional. Comment on what this implies about the relationship between minimal sufficient statistic dimension and parameter dimension.

topics.feynmanText:
A **sufficient statistic** keeps everything in the data that is informative about θ. But there can be many sufficient statistics — the entire sample is trivially sufficient, and so is any one-to-one transformation of any sufficient statistic. The question becomes: what is the *most compressed* sufficient statistic? That is the **minimal sufficient statistic** — a sufficient statistic that is a function of every other sufficient statistic.

To check whether a candidate T is minimal sufficient, you can use a clean criterion due to Lehmann and Scheffé. Look at the **likelihood ratio** L(θ; x) / L(θ; y) for two sample points x and y. If this ratio is **independent of θ exactly when T(x) = T(y)**, then T is minimal sufficient. Intuitively: two samples that produce the same value of T are "indistinguishable" from the parameter's point of view, and samples that produce different values of T are distinguishable. T captures every distinction the parameter can detect, and no more.

The most common pattern in exam questions is the **exponential family**. Distributions like Bernoulli, Poisson, Normal, Gamma, and Beta have densities that can be written

f(x; θ) = h(x) · c(θ) · exp(η(θ)' T(x)).

In an exponential family, T(X) = (Σᵢ T₁(Xᵢ), ..., Σᵢ Tₖ(Xᵢ)) is **always** minimal sufficient for the natural parameter — the Lehmann–Scheffé criterion is automatically satisfied.

The trickier cases are non-exponential-family distributions, especially when the **support depends on the parameter** (e.g. U(0, θ)). For those you have to compute the likelihood ratio directly, often involving indicator functions, and read off T from the condition that the ratio is parameter-free.
feynmanKeyTerms: independent of θ exactly when T(x) = T(y)

4. Argue both directions:
   (i) T(x) = T(y) ⇒ ratio is free of θ;
   (ii) ratio free of θ ⇒ T(x) = T(y).
   - For exponential families (i) is by substitution
     and (ii) uses η(θ) varying.
   - For support-dependent families (i) and (ii) come from
     comparing indicator functions.
5. Conclude: by the Lehmann–Scheffé criterion, T(X) is minimal sufficient.
6. (If asked) note completeness and the resulting UMVUE.

topics.title: ST2134 - Sufficient Statistics (Exam Sheet)

sections.0:
Suppose you have 1,000 coin flips and you want to know if the coin is biased. Do you need to remember every single flip in order? No -- all you need is the total number of heads. That total captures *everything* the 1,000 flips can tell you about the coin's bias. The individual sequence (HHTHT...) adds no extra information once you know the total. That total is a **sufficient statistic**.

Sufficiency means: once you know the value of the sufficient statistic, learning the rest of the data doesn't help you estimate the parameter any better. It's the ultimate data compression -- you throw away the raw data and keep only the summary, with zero loss of information about the parameter.

The **factorisation theorem** gives you a mechanical test: if you can split the joint probability of your data into one piece that depends on the parameter and the data only through T, and another piece that doesn't depend on the parameter at all, then T is sufficient. It's like saying: all the "parameter signal" lives inside T; everything else is pure noise.

The **Rao-Blackwell theorem** tells you why sufficiency matters for estimation: if you have any estimator, you can always improve it (or at least not make it worse) by conditioning it on a sufficient statistic. Start with a crude estimator like X₁ (just the first observation), condition on the sufficient statistic, and you get the sample mean -- a better estimator. Sufficiency is the engine that drives estimator improvement.
sections.1:
**Purpose:** A sufficient statistic captures all the information in the sample about the parameter. Once you know the sufficient statistic, the remaining data tells you nothing more about \(\theta\). The factorisation theorem is the standard tool for proving sufficiency.

**Key definitions:**

| Term | Formal definition |
|---|---|
| **Sufficient statistic** | \(T(\mathbf{X})\) is sufficient for \(\theta\) if \(P(\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{x} \mid T = t)\) does not depend on \(\theta\) |
| **Factorisation theorem** | \(T(\mathbf{X})\) is sufficient for \(\theta\) iff \(f(\mathbf{x}; \theta) = g(T(\mathbf{x}), \theta) \cdot h(\mathbf{x})\) |
| **Minimal sufficient** | A sufficient statistic that is a function of every other sufficient statistic — achieves greatest data reduction |
| **Lehmann-Scheffé criterion** | \(T\) is minimal sufficient iff \(\frac{f(\mathbf{x};\theta)}{f(\mathbf{y};\theta)}\) is free of \(\theta \iff T(\mathbf{x}) = T(\mathbf{y})\) |
| **Sufficiency principle** | Inference about \(\theta\) should depend on data only through sufficient statistics |

**Why it matters:** The MLE is always a function of sufficient statistics. The Rao-Blackwell theorem uses sufficiency to improve estimators. The LR test can be expressed through sufficient statistics.
sections.2:
### Standard Sufficient Statistics (Must Memorise)

| Distribution | Sufficient statistic for \(\theta\) | Via factorisation |
|---|---|---|
| Bernoulli(\(\pi\)) | \(\sum X_i\) | \(\pi^{\sum x_i}(1-\pi)^{n-\sum x_i} \cdot 1\) |
| Bin(\(m, \pi\)), \(m\) known | \(\sum X_i\) | Same structure |
| Poisson(\(\lambda\)) | \(\sum X_i\) | \(e^{-n\lambda}\lambda^{\sum x_i} \cdot \prod(1/x_i!)\) |
| \(N(\mu, \sigma^2)\), \(\sigma^2\) known | \(\sum X_i\) (or \(\bar X\)) | Exponent splits into \(\mu\)-terms and remainder |
| \(N(\mu, \sigma^2)\), both unknown | \((\sum X_i, \sum X_i^2)\) | Two-dimensional |
| Exp(\(\lambda\)) | \(\sum X_i\) | \(\lambda^n e^{-\lambda\sum x_i} \cdot 1\) |
| Gamma(\(\alpha, \beta\)), \(\alpha\) known | \(\sum X_i\) | \(\beta^{n\alpha} e^{-\beta\sum x_i} \cdot \prod x_i^{\alpha-1}/\Gamma(\alpha)^n\) |
| Uniform\([0, \theta]\) | \(X_{(n)} = \max(X_i)\) | \(\theta^{-n} \cdot \mathbb{1}(x_{(n)} \leq \theta) \cdot 1\) |
| Shifted Exp: \(\lambda e^{-\lambda(x-\theta)}\), \(x \geq \theta\) | See below (depends on which parameter) | Requires indicator functions |

### The Indicator Function Rule (Critical for Support-Dependent Distributions)

When the support depends on \(\theta\), the sufficient statistic involves an **indicator function**. The examiner specifically tests this.

**Shifted Exponential:** \(f(x; \lambda, \theta) = \lambda e^{-\lambda(x-\theta)}\) for \(x \geq \theta\).

- For \(\lambda\) (with \(\theta\) known): \(T = \sum X_i\) is sufficient.
- For \(\theta\) (with \(\lambda\) known): \(T = X_{(1)} = \min(X_i)\) is sufficient.
  - Joint pdf: \(\lambda^n e^{-\lambda\sum(x_i - \theta)} \cdot \prod \mathbb{1}(x_i \geq \theta)\)
  - Key: \(\prod \mathbb{1}(x_i \geq \theta) = \mathbb{1}(x_{(1)} \geq \theta)\)
  - So: \(g(x_{(1)}, \theta) = \lambda^n e^{n\lambda\theta} \cdot \mathbb{1}(x_{(1)} \geq \theta)\) and \(h(\mathbf{x}) = e^{-\lambda\sum x_i}\)
- For \((\lambda, \theta)\) jointly: \(T = (\sum X_i, X_{(1)})\) is sufficient.

This pattern was tested: 2020 Q2b, 2023 Q1a — same distribution, same structure.

### The Rao-Blackwell Theorem

If \(\tilde\theta\) is any unbiased estimator and \(T\) is sufficient for \(\theta\), define:

\[
\hat\theta^* = E[\tilde\theta \mid T]
\]

Then:
1. \(\hat\theta^*\) is unbiased: \(E[\hat\theta^*] = \theta\)
2. \(\text{Var}(\hat\theta^*) \leq \text{Var}(\tilde\theta)\) for all \(\theta\)
3. \(\hat\theta^*\) is a function of \(T\) alone

**Proof uses:** Law of iterated expectations (\(E[E[Y|T]] = E[Y]\)) and conditional variance formula (\(\text{Var}(Y) = E[\text{Var}(Y|T)] + \text{Var}(E[Y|T]) \geq \text{Var}(E[Y|T])\)).

**Classic application:** Start with \(\tilde\mu = X_1\). Sufficient statistic: \(S = \sum X_i\). By symmetry of iid sample: \(E[X_1 \mid \sum X_i = s] = s/n = \bar X\). So the Rao-Blackwellized estimator is \(\bar X\), which has smaller variance than \(X_1\).
sections.3:
**Setup:** \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) iid with \(f(x; \lambda, \theta) = \lambda e^{-\lambda(x - \theta)}\) for \(x \geq \theta\), \(\lambda > 0\), \(\theta \in \mathbb{R}\).

**Part (i) — Sufficient statistic for \(\lambda\) when \(\theta\) is known (3 marks):**

Joint pdf: \(\lambda^n e^{-\lambda\sum(x_i - \theta)} \cdot \prod \mathbb{1}(x_i \geq \theta)\)

Since \(\theta\) is known, the indicator is a known constant. Factor as:

\(g(\sum x_i, \lambda) = \lambda^n e^{-\lambda(\sum x_i - n\theta)}\) and \(h(\mathbf{x}) = \prod \mathbb{1}(x_i \geq \theta)\)

By factorisation theorem, \(T = \sum X_i\) is sufficient for \(\lambda\).

**Part (ii) — Sufficient statistic for \(\theta\) when \(\lambda\) is known (4 marks):**

Now \(\lambda\) is fixed. The joint pdf is:

\(\lambda^n e^{-\lambda\sum x_i} \cdot e^{n\lambda\theta} \cdot \prod \mathbb{1}(x_i \geq \theta)\)

Key step: \(\prod_{i=1}^n \mathbb{1}(x_i \geq \theta) = \mathbb{1}(\min(x_i) \geq \theta) = \mathbb{1}(x_{(1)} \geq \theta)\)

Factor as: \(g(x_{(1)}, \theta) = e^{n\lambda\theta} \cdot \mathbb{1}(x_{(1)} \geq \theta)\) and \(h(\mathbf{x}) = \lambda^n e^{-\lambda\sum x_i}\)

By factorisation theorem, \(T = X_{(1)}\) is sufficient for \(\theta\).

**Part (iii) — Joint sufficient statistic (3 marks):**

Both \(\lambda\) and \(\theta\) unknown: \(T = (\sum X_i, X_{(1)})\) is jointly sufficient for \((\lambda, \theta)\).
sections.4:
**Statement:** Let \(\tilde\theta\) be an unbiased estimator of \(\theta\) and \(T\) sufficient for \(\theta\). Define \(\hat\theta^* = E[\tilde\theta \mid T]\). Then:
- \(E[\hat\theta^*] = \theta\) (unbiased)
- \(\text{Var}(\hat\theta^*) \leq \text{Var}(\tilde\theta)\)

**Proof — Unbiasedness:**

By iterated expectations: \(E[\hat\theta^*] = E[E[\tilde\theta \mid T]] = E[\tilde\theta] = \theta\).

**Proof — Variance reduction:**

By the law of total variance:

\(\text{Var}(\tilde\theta) = E[\text{Var}(\tilde\theta \mid T)] + \text{Var}(E[\tilde\theta \mid T])\)

Since \(\text{Var}(\tilde\theta \mid T) \geq 0\):

\(\text{Var}(\tilde\theta) \geq \text{Var}(E[\tilde\theta \mid T]) = \text{Var}(\hat\theta^*)\)
sections.5:
1. **(5 min)** State the factorisation theorem. Define sufficient statistic and minimal sufficient statistic.
2. **(10 min)** \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\). Show \(\sum X_i\) is sufficient for \(\lambda\) using the factorisation theorem.
3. **(12 min)** \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \sim \text{Uniform}[0, \theta]\). Show \(X_{(n)}\) is sufficient for \(\theta\). Explain why the indicator function \(\mathbb{1}(x_{(n)} \leq \theta)\) is essential. (Tested in multiple papers)
4. **(12 min)** \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) iid with \(f(x; \lambda, \theta) = \lambda e^{-\lambda(x-\theta)}\), \(x \geq \theta\). Find the sufficient statistic for (a) \(\lambda\) alone, (b) \(\theta\) alone, (c) \((\lambda, \theta)\) jointly.
5. **(8 min)** State the Rao-Blackwell theorem. Apply it: start with \(\tilde\mu = X_1\), condition on the sufficient statistic \(\sum X_i\), to show that \(\bar X\) is the improved estimator.
6. **(3 min)** The examiner says "state the factorisation theorem." Write a model answer. Do NOT provide the proof — the examiner warns this wastes time.
sections.6:
```
1. JOINT PDF/PMF: Write f(x₁,...,xₙ; θ) = ∏ f(xᵢ; θ).
   State the support explicitly. Use correct subscript notation.

2. SIMPLIFY: Combine terms. Collect all θ-dependent terms.
   If support depends on θ, express as indicator function.

3. FACTORISE: Write as g(T(x), θ) × h(x).
   - g contains ALL θ-dependent terms and data only through T.
   - h contains all terms free of θ.

4. INDICATOR STEP (if needed): Show that
   ∏ 𝟙(xᵢ ≥ θ) = 𝟙(x₍₁₎ ≥ θ) or
   ∏ 𝟙(xᵢ ≤ θ) = 𝟙(x₍ₙ₎ ≤ θ).

5. CONCLUDE: "By the factorisation theorem, T(X) = [expression]
   is sufficient for θ."
   State WHICH PARAMETER T is sufficient for.
```
sections.7:
1. **Incomplete factorisation.** Not clearly separating \(g(T, \theta)\) from \(h(\mathbf{x})\). Every term in \(g\) must depend on \(\mathbf{x}\) only through \(T\). Every term in \(h\) must be free of \(\theta\).

2. **Forgetting the indicator function.** When the support depends on \(\theta\) (Uniform\([0,\theta]\), shifted exponential), omitting \(\mathbb{1}(x_{(n)} \leq \theta)\) or \(\mathbb{1}(x_{(1)} \geq \theta)\) is a fatal error. The examiner flags this in commentaries for 2020, 2022, and 2023.

3. **Not stating which parameter the statistic is sufficient for.** The examiner says: "When deriving a sufficient statistic, also state the parameter to which it corresponds." Writing "\(T = \sum X_i\) is sufficient" without saying "for \(\lambda\)" loses marks.

4. **Confusing sufficiency with unbiasedness.** A sufficient statistic need not be an unbiased estimator. \(X_{(n)}\) is sufficient for \(\theta\) in Uniform\([0,\theta]\) but is biased (since \(E[X_{(n)}] = n\theta/(n+1) \neq \theta\)).

5. **Over-answering "state the theorem" questions.** The examiner explicitly warns: "When asked to state a theorem, do not give the proof as well — this simply wastes time." State the theorem cleanly and stop.

6. **Not using the law of iterated expectations in Rao-Blackwell.** The proof requires \(E[E[\tilde\theta | T]] = E[\tilde\theta]\). Not citing this identity costs marks in proof questions.

topics.feynmanText:
Suppose you have 1,000 coin flips and you want to know if the coin is biased. Do you need to remember every single flip in order? No -- all you need is the total number of heads. That total captures *everything* the 1,000 flips can tell you about the coin's bias. The individual sequence (HHTHT...) adds no extra information once you know the total. That total is a **sufficient statistic**.

Sufficiency means: once you know the value of the sufficient statistic, learning the rest of the data doesn't help you estimate the parameter any better. It's the ultimate data compression -- you throw away the raw data and keep only the summary, with zero loss of information about the parameter.

The **factorisation theorem** gives you a mechanical test: if you can split the joint probability of your data into one piece that depends on the parameter and the data only through T, and another piece that doesn't depend on the parameter at all, then T is sufficient. It's like saying: all the "parameter signal" lives inside T; everything else is pure noise.

The **Rao-Blackwell theorem** tells you why sufficiency matters for estimation: if you have any estimator, you can always improve it (or at least not make it worse) by conditioning it on a sufficient statistic. Start with a crude estimator like X₁ (just the first observation), condition on the sufficient statistic, and you get the sample mean -- a better estimator. Sufficiency is the engine that drives estimator improvement.
topics.modelAnswer:
1. **(5 min)** State the factorisation theorem. Define sufficient statistic and minimal sufficient statistic.
2. **(10 min)** \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\). Show \(\sum X_i\) is sufficient for \(\lambda\) using the factorisation theorem.
3. **(12 min)** \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \sim \text{Uniform}[0, \theta]\). Show \(X_{(n)}\) is sufficient for \(\theta\). Explain why the indicator function \(\mathbb{1}(x_{(n)} \leq \theta)\) is essential. (Tested in multiple papers)
4. **(12 min)** \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) iid with \(f(x; \lambda, \theta) = \lambda e^{-\lambda(x-\theta)}\), \(x \geq \theta\). Find the sufficient statistic for (a) \(\lambda\) alone, (b) \(\theta\) alone, (c) \((\lambda, \theta)\) jointly.
5. **(8 min)** State the Rao-Blackwell theorem. Apply it: start with \(\tilde\mu = X_1\), condition on the sufficient statistic \(\sum X_i\), to show that \(\bar X\) is the improved estimator.
6. **(3 min)** The examiner says "state the factorisation theorem." Write a model answer. Do NOT provide the proof — the examiner warns this wastes time.
topics.commonTraps:
```
1. JOINT PDF/PMF: Write f(x₁,...,xₙ; θ) = ∏ f(xᵢ; θ).
   State the support explicitly. Use correct subscript notation.

2. SIMPLIFY: Combine terms. Collect all θ-dependent terms.
   If support depends on θ, express as indicator function.

3. FACTORISE: Write as g(T(x), θ) × h(x).
   - g contains ALL θ-dependent terms and data only through T.
   - h contains all terms free of θ.

4. INDICATOR STEP (if needed): Show that
   ∏ 𝟙(xᵢ ≥ θ) = 𝟙(x₍₁₎ ≥ θ) or
   ∏ 𝟙(xᵢ ≤ θ) = 𝟙(x₍ₙ₎ ≤ θ).

5. CONCLUDE: "By the factorisation theorem, T(X) = [expression]
   is sufficient for θ."
   State WHICH PARAMETER T is sufficient for.
```

topics.title: ST2134 - Optimal Estimators and Tests (Exam Sheet)

sections.0:
This topic asks two beautiful questions: what is the *best possible* estimate, and what is the *best possible* test?

For estimation, the **Cramér-Rao lower bound** says there's a speed limit on accuracy. No unbiased estimator can have variance below 1/I(θ), where I(θ) is the Fisher information. It's a law of nature: the data only contain so much information, and no clever trick can squeeze out more than what's there. An estimator that actually hits this speed limit is called "efficient" -- it extracts every last drop of information from the data.

For testing, the **Neyman-Pearson lemma** answers: if you're choosing between two specific hypotheses, the best possible test (highest power for a given size) is to look at the likelihood ratio. You compute how much more plausible the data are under H₁ than under H₀. If that ratio is big enough, reject H₀. No other test of the same size can have higher power -- it's mathematically impossible.

The beautiful connection: just as the Cramér-Rao bound sets a floor on estimator variance, the Neyman-Pearson lemma sets a ceiling on test power. Both are optimality results that tell you the best you can possibly do, and both are achievable.

A **UMP test** extends Neyman-Pearson to composite alternatives: if the most powerful test happens to be the *same test* regardless of which specific alternative value you're considering, then that test is uniformly most powerful -- the best against every alternative simultaneously. This works for one-sided tests in exponential families, but for two-sided tests, UMP usually doesn't exist, and you fall back to the likelihood ratio test.
sections.1:
**Purpose:** This topic answers two fundamental questions: (1) What is the best possible unbiased estimator? (2) What is the best possible test? The Cramér-Rao bound gives a lower limit on estimator variance. The Neyman-Pearson lemma identifies the most powerful test.

**Key definitions:**

| Term | Formal definition |
|---|---|
| **CRLB** | \(\text{Var}(\hat\theta) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)}\) for unbiased \(\hat\theta\); more generally \(\text{Var}(T) \geq \frac{[g'(\theta)]^2}{I(\theta)}\) for \(E(T) = g(\theta)\) |
| **Efficient estimator** | An unbiased estimator achieving the CRLB — i.e., with \(\text{Var} = 1/I(\theta)\) |
| **MVUE** | Minimum variance unbiased estimator — smallest variance among all unbiased estimators |
| **Most powerful test** | A test of size \(\alpha\) that maximises power against a specific alternative |
| **UMP test** | Uniformly most powerful — most powerful against every alternative in \(\Theta_1\) |
| **Neyman-Pearson lemma** | For simple \(H_0\) vs simple \(H_1\): the MP test rejects when \(L(\theta_1)/L(\theta_0) \geq k\) |
sections.2:
### Cramér-Rao Inequality

**For unbiased estimator \(\hat\theta\) of \(\theta\):**

\[
\text{Var}(\hat\theta) \geq \frac{1}{I(\theta)} = \frac{1}{n \cdot i(\theta)}
\]

**For unbiased estimator \(T\) of \(g(\theta)\):**

\[
\text{Var}(T) \geq \frac{[g'(\theta)]^2}{I(\theta)}
\]

### When is the CRLB Attained?

The bound is attained (equality holds) iff the score function can be written as:

\[
U(\theta) = a(\theta)[T(\mathbf{X}) - g(\theta)]
\]

for some function \(a(\theta)\). If this holds, \(T\) is the unique MVUE of \(g(\theta)\).

**Practical check:** Compute the score function. If it is a linear function of some statistic \(T\), then \(T\) (suitably scaled) attains the CRLB.

### CRLB Proof Sketch (Tested 2023 Q1b — 10 marks)

Uses the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality**:

\[[\text{Cov}(T, U)]^2 \leq \text{Var}(T) \cdot \text{Var}(U)\]

Key steps:
1. Differentiate \(E_\theta[T(\mathbf{X})] = g(\theta)\) under the integral sign to get \(\text{Cov}(T, U) = g'(\theta)\).
2. Use \(\text{Var}(U) = I(\theta)\) and \(E[U] = 0\).
3. Apply Cauchy-Schwarz: \([g'(\theta)]^2 \leq \text{Var}(T) \cdot I(\theta)\).
4. Rearrange: \(\text{Var}(T) \geq [g'(\theta)]^2 / I(\theta)\).

### Neyman-Pearson Lemma

**Setup:** Simple \(H_0: \theta = \theta_0\) vs simple \(H_1: \theta = \theta_1\).

**Statement:** The most powerful test of size \(\alpha\) has rejection region:

\[
C^* = \left\{\mathbf{x} : \frac{L(\theta_1; \mathbf{x})}{L(\theta_0; \mathbf{x})} \geq k\right\}
\]

where \(k\) is chosen so that \(P_{\theta_0}(\mathbf{X} \in C^*) = \alpha\).

### Extending to UMP Tests

A test is **UMP** of size \(\alpha\) for \(H_0: \theta = \theta_0\) vs \(H_1: \theta \in \Theta_1\) if:
1. The MP rejection region from N-P does **not depend** on the specific \(\theta_1 \in \Theta_1\)
2. The size condition holds: \(P_{\theta_0}(\mathbf{X} \in C^*) \leq \alpha\)

**When UMP tests exist:** One-parameter exponential families with one-sided alternatives (monotone likelihood ratio property).

**When UMP tests do NOT exist:** Two-sided alternatives (\(H_1: \theta \neq \theta_0\)) for most families. Use the LR test instead.
sections.3:
**Setup:** \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \sim \text{Gamma}(k, \alpha)\) with \(k\) known. MLE: \(\hat\alpha = k/\bar X\).

**Step 1 — Fisher information:**

\(i(\alpha) = k/\alpha^2\), so \(I(\alpha) = nk/\alpha^2\).

**Step 2 — CRLB for unbiased estimator of \(\alpha\):**

\(\text{Var}(\hat\alpha) \geq \frac{1}{I(\alpha)} = \frac{\alpha^2}{nk}\)

**Step 3 — Does the MLE attain the CRLB?**

Check the score: \(U(\alpha) = \frac{nk}{\alpha} - \sum X_i = \frac{nk}{\alpha}\left(1 - \frac{\bar X}{k/\alpha}\right)\)

This is \(\frac{nk}{\alpha}(1 - \alpha\bar X/k)\). Not quite in the form \(a(\alpha)(\hat\alpha - \alpha)\), so the MLE of \(\alpha\) does NOT attain the CRLB exactly (it attains it only asymptotically).

**However:** The MLE of \(1/\alpha\) (i.e., \(\bar X/k\)) IS unbiased and the score is \(U = \frac{nk}{\alpha}(1 - \alpha \cdot \bar X/k)\), which has the form \(a(\alpha)[T - g(\alpha)]\) with \(T = \bar X/k\) and \(g(\alpha) = 1/\alpha\). So \(\bar X/k\) is the MVUE of \(1/\alpha\) and attains the CRLB.
sections.4:
**Setup:** \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \sim N(0, \sigma^2)\). Test \(H_0: \sigma^2 = \sigma_0^2\) vs \(H_1: \sigma^2 = \sigma_1^2\) where \(\sigma_1^2 > \sigma_0^2\).

**Step 1 — Likelihood ratio:**

\[
\frac{L(\sigma_1^2)}{L(\sigma_0^2)} = \left(\frac{\sigma_0}{\sigma_1}\right)^n \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sigma_1^2} - \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2}\right)\sum X_i^2\right]
\]

**Step 2 — Monotonicity:**

Since \(\sigma_1^2 > \sigma_0^2\), the coefficient \(-\frac{1}{2}(1/\sigma_1^2 - 1/\sigma_0^2) > 0\), so the ratio is **increasing** in \(\sum X_i^2\).

Therefore: \(\frac{L(\sigma_1^2)}{L(\sigma_0^2)} \geq k \iff \sum X_i^2 \geq c\).

**Step 3 — Find \(c\):**

Under \(H_0\): \(\sum X_i^2/\sigma_0^2 \sim \chi^2_n\), so \(c = \sigma_0^2 \chi^2_{n, \alpha}\).

**Step 4 — UMP property:**

The rejection region \(\{\sum X_i^2 \geq c\}\) does **not depend on \(\sigma_1^2\)** (only requires \(\sigma_1^2 > \sigma_0^2\)). Therefore the same test is MP for every \(\sigma_1^2 > \sigma_0^2\), making it **UMP** for:
- \(H_0: \sigma^2 = \sigma_0^2\) vs \(H_1: \sigma^2 > \sigma_0^2\)
sections.5:
**Setup:** \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) with \(f(x; \beta) = \frac{1}{6\beta^4}x^3 e^{-x/\beta}\) for \(x > 0\). Given \(E(X) = 4\beta\).

**Score function:** \(U(\beta) = -\frac{4n}{\beta} + \frac{\sum X_i}{\beta^2} = \frac{n}{\beta^2}\left(\frac{\sum X_i}{n} - 4\beta\right) = \frac{n}{\beta^2}(\bar X - 4\beta)\)

This has the form \(a(\beta)[T - g(\beta)]\) with \(T = \bar X\), \(g(\beta) = 4\beta\), \(a(\beta) = n/\beta^2\).

So \(\bar X\) is the MVUE of \(4\beta\), and by linearity, \(\hat\beta = \bar X/4\) is the MVUE of \(\beta\).

**Verify:** CRLB for \(\beta\): \(\text{Var}(\hat\beta) \geq [g'(\beta)]^2/I(\beta)\). With \(g(\beta) = \beta\), \(g'=1\), \(I(\beta) = 4n/\beta^2\): CRLB = \(\beta^2/(4n)\).

Actual variance: \(\text{Var}(\bar X/4) = \text{Var}(X)/(16n) = 4\beta^2/(16n) = \beta^2/(4n)\). Equals the CRLB — confirmed MVUE.
sections.6:
1. **(8 min)** State the Cramér-Rao inequality for (a) unbiased estimator of \(\theta\), (b) unbiased estimator of \(g(\theta)\). State the condition for attainment.
2. **(12 min)** Prove the CRLB using Cauchy-Schwarz. (Tested 2023 Q1b — 10 marks)
3. **(10 min)** \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)\). MLE is \(\hat\lambda = 1/\bar X\). Fisher info: \(I(\lambda) = n/\lambda^2\). Check whether the MLE of \(\lambda\) attains the CRLB. Check whether \(\bar X\) attains the CRLB for \(1/\lambda\).
4. **(10 min)** State the Neyman-Pearson lemma. For \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)\) with \(\sigma^2\) known, derive the MP test of \(H_0: \mu = \mu_0\) vs \(H_1: \mu = \mu_1\) where \(\mu_1 > \mu_0\). Show it is UMP against \(H_1: \mu > \mu_0\).
5. **(10 min)** State and prove the Rao-Blackwell theorem. (Tested 2022 Q1c — 12 marks)
sections.7:
### For CRLB Questions

```
1. FISHER INFO: Compute I(θ) = n·i(θ).

2. CRLB: State Var(T) ≥ [g'(θ)]² / I(θ) for E(T) = g(θ).
   If g(θ) = θ, the bound is 1/I(θ).

3. ACTUAL VARIANCE: Compute Var(θ̂) from the distribution.

4. COMPARE: Does Var = CRLB?
   If YES: "θ̂ is efficient / is the MVUE of g(θ)."
   If NO: "θ̂ does not attain the CRLB."
   Note: MLE always attains CRLB asymptotically.

5. SCORE CHECK (optional): If score = a(θ)[T - g(θ)],
   then T is the MVUE of g(θ) and attains the CRLB.
```

### For Neyman-Pearson Questions

```
1. STATE: H₀: θ = θ₀ (simple) vs H₁: θ = θ₁ (simple).

2. LIKELIHOOD RATIO: Write L(θ₁)/L(θ₀) explicitly.

3. SIMPLIFY: Reduce to a function of a known statistic.
   Show the ratio is ≥ k iff T(X) ≥ c (or ≤ c).

4. DISTRIBUTION UNDER H₀: State the distribution of T|H₀.

5. CRITICAL VALUE: Choose c so that P_{θ₀}(T ∈ C) = α.

6. UMP (if applicable): If C does not depend on θ₁,
   the test is UMP for the composite alternative.
```
sections.8:
1. **Not connecting CRLB to Cauchy-Schwarz in the proof.** The proof's key step is: differentiate \(E[T] = g(\theta)\) under the integral sign to get \(\text{Cov}(T, U) = g'(\theta)\). Then apply C-S. Skipping the differentiation step loses marks.

2. **Claiming the MLE always attains the CRLB.** The MLE attains it **asymptotically**, not necessarily for finite \(n\). For example, the MLE of \(\alpha\) in Gamma(\(k, \alpha\)) does not attain the CRLB for finite \(n\), but the MLE of \(1/\alpha\) does.

3. **Not correctly defining the rejection region in N-P.** The rejection region is \(\{L(\theta_1)/L(\theta_0) \geq k\}\), not \(\leq k\). Large likelihood ratios favour \(H_1\).

4. **Claiming UMP exists for two-sided alternatives.** For \(H_1: \theta \neq \theta_0\), UMP tests generally do not exist (the MP test for \(\theta_1 > \theta_0\) differs from the MP test for \(\theta_1 < \theta_0\)). Use the LR test instead.

5. **Writing overly long proofs.** The examiner warns: "When asked to state a theorem, do not give the proof as well." And when asked to prove, follow the Subject Guide proof structure — do not improvise a longer proof.

6. **Forgetting the score-function characterisation of MVUE.** If the score equals \(a(\theta)(T - g(\theta))\), then \(T\) is the MVUE of \(g(\theta)\). This is the fastest way to check CRLB attainment.

topics.title: ST2134 - Proof Fluency Day (Exam Sheet)

sections.1:
- Improve structure, notation discipline, and step logic.
- Reduce avoidable algebra slips.
- Write concise but complete proofs under time pressure.
sections.2:
1. State claim and assumptions.  
2. Define notation and known results used.  
3. Proceed in small logical steps (one line = one claim).  
4. Finish with explicit conclusion marker.
sections.3:
- 20 min: short derivation proof  
- 20 min: sufficiency/likelihood proof fragment  
- 20 min: test-property argument in prose + math
sections.4:
- Logical flow (0-2)
- Correct notation (0-2)
- Correct math steps (0-3)
- Clear conclusion (0-1)
- Time control (0-2)
sections.5:
- Jumping steps and losing logical chain.
- Not stating assumptions upfront.
- Spending too long polishing first proof.

topics.modelAnswer:
- Jumping steps and losing logical chain.
- Not stating assumptions upfront.
- Spending too long polishing first proof.

sections.1:
- Integrate all core blocks under exam timing.
- Test retrieval speed, not just understanding.
- Identify final recurring weak links before mock #2.
sections.2:
1. 45-60 min mixed set (sampling, estimation, testing, likelihood).  
2. 30 min mark + classify mistakes.  
3. 20 min re-attempt worst error without notes.
sections.3:
- Accuracy >= 78%
- Completion >= 90%
- <= 2 repeated error types
sections.4:
- Spending too long on one derivation.
- Leaving interpretation/conclusion lines blank.
- Doing correction without re-attempt.

sections.1:

sections.3:

- Completion >= 92%
- Proof clarity score improved from Mock #1
sections.4:

- Create concise trigger rules for each.
sections.5:

- Fatigue causing sloppy notation.
- Ignoring time-budget overruns.

sections.1:
